title: Horses
summary: The domestication of the horse around 3500 BCE on the Eurasian steppe was among the most consequential events in human history -- transforming warfare, trade, agriculture, and political organization across Eurasia, enabling the steppe empires that repeatedly reshaped the ancient and medieval world, and reverberating through the Columbian Exchange to transform the Americas.
hook: Before the horse, the fastest thing a human being could do was run. After the horse, a person could travel hundreds of miles in days, strike and retreat before an enemy could respond, and coordinate military action across vast distances. This single change -- domesticating an animal that could be ridden -- reorganized the military, political, and economic geography of Eurasia for five thousand years. The horse made the Mongol Empire possible. It carried the Indo-European languages across Eurasia. It transformed the Great Plains of North America after 1492. The internal combustion engine replaced it -- but we still measure engine power in horsepower.

beginner.theFoundation: The domestication of the horse was one of the most consequential events in human history. It happened on the Eurasian steppe around 3500 BCE. Before horses were domesticated, humans walked or used cattle and donkeys for transport. The horse made long-distance movement dramatically faster. It transformed warfare, trade, agriculture, and how societies were organized. Civilizations with horses could move and fight at speeds that civilizations without horses could not match. The horse shaped the ancient and medieval world as fundamentally as the internal combustion engine shaped the modern one.
beginner.howItArrived: Horses were first domesticated by steppe peoples in what is now Kazakhstan and Ukraine. The earliest domestication involved riding and milking. Wheeled vehicles pulled by horses appeared shortly after. The technology spread rapidly -- faster communication, more effective hunting, and new military capabilities all followed immediately. By 2000 BCE, horse-drawn chariots were transforming warfare across the ancient Near East. By 1000 BCE, cavalry -- soldiers fighting on horseback -- had become a decisive military force across Eurasia.
beginner.whatItReorganized: Horses reorganized the relationship between steppe peoples and settled civilizations. Steppe nomads on horseback could raid settled areas faster than those civilizations could respond. They could move armies hundreds of miles in days. This gave steppe peoples -- Scythians, Huns, Turks, Mongols -- military advantages that persisted for thousands of years. Horses also reorganized agriculture in Europe and the Americas. The horse collar, developed in medieval Europe, made horses more useful than oxen for plowing. The introduction of horses to the Americas after 1492 transformed indigenous societies, particularly on the Great Plains.
beginner.thePoliticalEconomy: The political economy of horses was central to ancient and medieval states. States controlling horse-breeding regions had military advantages. China repeatedly fought wars to control Central Asian horse territory. The Arab military expansion was powered by horses. The Mongol Empire -- the largest land empire in history -- was built entirely on horseback. In medieval Europe, the horse determined social hierarchy: knights were defined by their ability to afford a warhorse and armor. The word chivalry comes from the French word for horse. Horse ownership and social status were inseparable.
beginner.theFeedback: The feedback effects of horse domestication were multiple. Horses enabled the Indo-European migrations that spread a family of languages -- including English, Spanish, Russian, Persian, and Sanskrit -- across Eurasia. When horses were brought to the Americas after 1492, indigenous populations suffered devastating epidemics partly linked to the new animal contacts the Columbian Exchange created. Horses also contributed to ecological change -- overgrazing in the steppe transformed grassland ecosystems across Eurasia over millennia.
beginner.presentAndFuture: The horse role was transformed by industrialization. Internal combustion engines replaced horses for transportation and agriculture in the 20th century. The term horsepower -- still used to measure engine output -- marks this transition. Today horses are kept primarily for sport, leisure, and ceremonial purposes. Their historical significance is permanent: the horse shaped the political geography of the ancient and medieval world in ways that still structure the present. The Eurasian steppe, which horse-riding peoples made the military pivot of the continent, is now a region whose borders and ethnic compositions reflect five millennia of horse-enabled history.

general.theFoundation: David Anthony's The Horse, the Wheel, and Language (2007) is the foundational modern account of horse domestication and its consequences. Drawing on archaeological evidence from the Pontic-Caspian steppe -- including bone chemistry evidence of horse milking and bit wear on teeth indicating riding -- Anthony places the first domestication in the Botai culture of Kazakhstan around 3500 BCE. More controversially, Anthony connects horse domestication to the expansion of Proto-Indo-European languages -- the ancestor of the language family that includes English, Spanish, Russian, Persian, and Sanskrit. On this account, horse-riding steppe peoples spread their languages across Eurasia through migration and cultural influence, producing the extraordinary geographic spread of the Indo-European language family.
general.howItArrived: The horse-drawn chariot represents the first decisive military application of horse technology. Chariots appeared across the ancient Near East and Central Asia around 2000-1800 BCE, transforming warfare by combining mobility and firepower in a way that infantry could not counter. The chariot was the tank of the Bronze Age: expensive, technically demanding, and militarily decisive. Egyptian, Hittite, Mycenaean, and Shang Chinese militaries were organized around chariot forces. Robert Drews's The End of the Bronze Age argues that the collapse of chariot-based militaries around 1200 BCE -- replaced by infantry using iron weapons -- was the primary driver of the Bronze Age Collapse, one of the most catastrophic civilizational collapses in history.
general.whatItReorganized: The relationship between steppe nomads and settled civilizations represents one of the most persistent structural dynamics in Eurasian history. Nomadic horse-riding peoples had specific military advantages: mobility, the ability to strike and retreat, and endurance in logistically challenging environments. Settled civilizations had specific advantages: agricultural surplus, urban concentration, literacy, and the ability to organize large numbers of specialists. The dynamic tension between these two modes of organization -- which Owen Lattimore analyzed in Inner Asian Frontiers of China -- drove the history of Eurasia from the Bronze Age through the early modern period, when gunpowder weapons finally negated the steppe nomads' tactical advantages.
general.thePoliticalEconomy: The trade in horses was one of the most important commodity trades in the ancient and medieval world. China's chronic shortage of horses -- the Han, Tang, and Ming dynasties repeatedly needed cavalry horses for defense against steppe nomads -- drove an extensive trade from Central Asian suppliers, often exchanged for silk in the silk-for-horses trade that was one of the economic foundations of the Silk Road. The Arabian horse -- bred for speed and endurance in desert conditions -- was the foundation of the Arab military expansion and became the most prized breed in the medieval world. Understanding the political economy of horses means understanding why states fought to control horse-breeding territory and why horse traders occupied a distinctive and lucrative niche in ancient and medieval economies.
general.theFeedback: Pita Kelekna's The Horse in Human History provides the most comprehensive account of how horse domestication shaped human societies across multiple dimensions simultaneously. Kelekna traces the horse's influence on language spread, military organization, religious practice (horse sacrifice was widespread across the Indo-European world), social hierarchy, and long-distance trade. The horse's influence on religion is particularly striking: horses appear in the ritual practices of Indo-European peoples from Ireland to India, and the horse sacrifice was a royal ritual across ancient Indo-European cultures. The deep cultural embedding of the horse in Indo-European societies reflects the animal's centrality to their social and economic life for millennia.
general.presentAndFuture: The Great Plains transformation after 1492 is one of the most dramatic examples of how horse introduction reshapes societies. Spanish horses, escaped or traded from Mexican colonies, spread northward across the Great Plains in the 17th century. Within a generation, Plains peoples who had been semi-sedentary farmers adapted to mounted buffalo hunting. Societies like the Lakota, Comanche, and Cheyenne reorganized around horse culture -- their social hierarchies, military capabilities, and material culture were transformed within a few generations. The Comanche, in particular, became the most militarily effective horse culture in the Americas and controlled a territory (Comancheria) that effectively blocked Spanish and later American expansion for over a century.

advanced.theFoundation: The hypothesis that horse domestication drove Indo-European language expansion has been substantially supported by ancient DNA research. Studies published in Nature from 2015 onward demonstrated large-scale population movements from the Pontic-Caspian steppe into Europe during the third millennium BCE -- movements that correlate with the spread of Indo-European languages and that are plausibly connected to horse-enabled mobility. Ancient DNA from the Yamnaya culture and related steppe populations shows that these people contributed massive genetic ancestry to Bronze Age European and South Asian populations. This genetic evidence substantially validates the steppe hypothesis for Indo-European origins, though the precise mechanisms of language spread remain debated.
advanced.howItArrived: The transition from chariot warfare to cavalry warfare -- which occurred across Eurasia during the first millennium BCE -- has been analyzed most carefully by Ann Hyland's work on ancient and medieval military horsemanship. The transition required not just the horse but specific technologies: the saddle (which provided stability for a mounted archer), and eventually the stirrup (which allowed cavalry to deliver mounted lance charges without being unseated). The stirrup debate -- whether the adoption of the stirrup in early medieval Europe drove the development of feudalism, as Lynn White argued in Medieval Technology and Social Change (1962) -- is one of the most contested questions in the history of technology, with the causation running in both directions or neither as different historians have argued.
advanced.whatItReorganized: The end of horse-based military power came through a combination of gunpowder weapons and economic change. Gunpowder firearms reduced the tactical advantage of cavalry in battle -- though cavalry remained militarily significant through the First World War, and horse-drawn artillery was essential to 19th-century armies. The deeper transformation was economic: the internal combustion engine made horses obsolete for agricultural and transportation purposes within a few decades of the early 20th century. The horse population in the United States peaked around 1915 and declined dramatically through the mid-20th century as tractors and trucks replaced horses in agriculture and transportation. The speed of this transition -- from ubiquity to marginality within a generation -- illustrates how completely material foundations can change when a superior technology arrives.
advanced.thePoliticalEconomy: The Comanche Empire -- analyzed most fully in Pekka Hamalainen's The Comanche Empire (2008) -- represents the most sophisticated application of horse culture in the Americas and a significant revision of the standard narrative of European expansion. The Comanche acquired horses in the late 17th century and within a generation had built the most militarily effective indigenous society in North America. At their peak in the early 19th century, the Comanche controlled approximately 240,000 square miles and systematically raided and extracted tribute from Spanish and Mexican settlements across the southern plains. Hamalainen presents the Comanche not as victims of European expansion but as imperial actors in their own right -- who shaped the trajectory of Texas and New Mexico history more fundamentally than the Spanish colonial system they operated alongside.
advanced.theFeedback: The relationship between horse domestication and the Indo-European language spread continues to be refined by ancient DNA research. Studies by David Reich, Pontus Skoglund, and their colleagues have demonstrated that Bronze Age steppe herders -- the Yamnaya culture -- contributed massive genetic ancestry to Bronze Age European and South Asian populations. The integration of archaeogenomics with linguistic and archaeological evidence is producing a more precise picture of how horse domestication, population movement, and language spread were connected -- though the causal mechanisms remain an active research frontier. Ludovic Orlando's group at the Centre for GeoGenetics has produced some of the most important ancient DNA research on horses specifically, documenting the replacement of early domestic horse populations by a single domesticated lineage in the Bronze Age that is ancestral to all modern domestic horses.
advanced.presentAndFuture: The horse's contemporary role -- primarily in sport, leisure, and ceremonial functions -- represents a dramatic transformation from its historical centrality. But the horse's historical significance continues to generate productive research. The study of ancient horse DNA -- extracted from archaeological specimens across Eurasia -- is providing new evidence about the history of horse domestication, the genetics of different breeds, and the relationship between specific horse populations and specific human cultures. Orlando's group has documented not just the replacement of early domestic horse populations but the specific genetic changes associated with the traits that made horses valuable as military animals -- speed, docility, and endurance.

foundation.theStory: The domestication of the horse around 3500 BCE on the Eurasian steppe transformed human history in ways that are still being fully understood. Horses enabled the steppe empires that repeatedly reshaped Eurasia, spread the Indo-European languages across the continent, reorganized the military and political geography of the ancient and medieval world, and -- through the Columbian Exchange -- transformed indigenous societies in the Americas. The internal combustion engine replaced the horse in the 20th century, but its influence on the world we live in is permanent.
foundation.theDebate: The central debate concerns the relationship between horse domestication and the spread of Indo-European languages. David Anthony's argument -- that horse-riding steppe peoples spread Proto-Indo-European across Eurasia through migration -- has been substantially supported by ancient DNA research. But the mechanisms of language spread (migration? elite diffusion? trade contact?) and the relative importance of horses versus wheeled vehicles in enabling that spread remain actively debated.
foundation.whyItMatters: Horses matter for understanding the Mongol Empire, the Indo-European language expansion, the dynamics of steppe-settled civilization interaction across Eurasian history, and the transformation of the Americas after 1492. They also matter as a case study in how a single animal domestication can reshape the military, political, economic, and cultural geography of an entire continent.
questions: David Anthony argues that horse domestication enabled the spread of Proto-Indo-European languages across Eurasia. Ancient DNA research has supported this by showing large-scale population movements from the Pontic steppe. But population movement does not necessarily mean language replacement. What additional evidence would help establish the causal mechanism?

The Comanche built an empire on the Great Plains after acquiring horses in the late 17th century. Pekka Hamalainen presents them as imperial actors who shaped the history of Texas and New Mexico as fundamentally as the Spanish colonial system. What does this case tell us about how quickly material foundation changes can reorganize political and social structures?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential scholarly works are David Anthony's The Horse, the Wheel, and Language (2007) -- the most comprehensive account of horse domestication and its linguistic consequences -- and Pita Kelekna's The Horse in Human History (2009), which provides the broadest account across multiple civilizations and time periods. Pekka Hamalainen's The Comanche Empire (2008) is the essential account of horse culture in the Americas. Robert Drews's The End of the Bronze Age analyzes the relationship between horse-based chariot warfare and the Bronze Age Collapse. Ancient DNA research (David Reich, Eske Willerslev, Ludovic Orlando) is progressively transforming the history of both horse and human population movements.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The archaeological evidence for horse domestication has been substantially enriched by ancient DNA analysis and bone chemistry analysis (which can identify milking and biting practices). These methods have substantially extended what can be known about early horse domestication. The evidence for social and cultural consequences in prehistoric contexts is less direct -- the relationship between material change (horse riding) and social change (language spread, political organization) must be inferred from correlations in the archaeological record rather than direct documentation.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible position on horse domestication must engage with the evidence for both the timing of domestication and the mechanisms of its spread. It must address the Indo-European language hypothesis -- now substantially supported by ancient DNA -- while being honest about the difference between population movement (which the DNA demonstrates) and language replacement (which requires additional argument). And it must address the comparative question of why horse domestication had such different consequences in different contexts.
questions: Lynn White argued in Medieval Technology and Social Change (1962) that the adoption of the stirrup in early medieval Europe drove the development of feudalism. This argument has been extensively criticized. What methodological issues does it raise about attributing social change to specific technological developments?

The Comanche Empire demonstrates that horse culture transformed indigenous Plains societies within a generation of acquiring horses. How do you distinguish the effects of a material foundation (the horse) from the effects of the specific historical context in which it arrived?

research.topic: The timing and location of initial horse domestication
research.content: Alan Outram's 2009 analysis of horse remains from the Botai culture in Kazakhstan -- examining bone fat residues indicating milk processing, and pathologies on horse teeth consistent with bit use -- provided the most direct evidence for horse domestication around 3500 BCE on the Pontic-Caspian steppe. Subsequent ancient DNA analysis complicated the picture by showing that Botai horses are not ancestral to modern domestic horses -- suggesting either a later separate domestication event or a complex replacement of early domestic populations. The current scholarly consensus places initial domestication on the Pontic-Caspian steppe around 3500-3000 BCE but treats the genetic relationship between early domestic and modern horses as an active research question.

research.topic: The relationship between horse domestication and Indo-European language spread
research.content: Ancient DNA research published from 2015 onward (Haak et al. 2015, Mathieson et al. 2015, and subsequent studies) has demonstrated large-scale population movements from the Pontic-Caspian steppe into Europe and South Asia during the third and second millennia BCE -- movements that correlate with the spread of Indo-European languages. This genetic evidence substantially supports David Anthony's steppe hypothesis for Indo-European origins. But the mechanisms of language spread remain debated: how did steppe populations' languages come to replace those of the numerically larger European and South Asian populations they moved into? The integration of archaeogenomics, linguistics, and archaeology is an active and rapidly developing research frontier.

research.topic: The subjective experience of the first horse riders and the social processes of early domestication
research.content: How horse domestication actually happened -- the specific interactions between early steppe peoples and wild horses, the social processes through which riding became accepted and spread, the experience of the first riders -- cannot be recovered from the available evidence. What evidence would resolve this: written accounts from the period of initial domestication, which did not exist. Why unavailable: horse domestication preceded the development of writing in the Pontic steppe by approximately 3,000 years. What can be responsibly inferred: from archaeological evidence of bone assemblages, bit wear, and settlement patterns, the general timeline and geographic location of domestication can be established with reasonable confidence. What must remain unknowable: the specific social and cognitive processes through which domestication happened, the role of individual actors versus gradual cultural evolution, and the experience of the transition from wild horse hunting to horse herding to horse riding.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Horse as Identity
comparativeNarrative.content: For steppe nomadic peoples -- from the Scythians through the Mongols to the Kazakhs and Mongols of today -- the horse was not merely a tool but an identity. Steppe cultures organized their social hierarchies around horse ownership, their religions around horse sacrifice, their literature around horse qualities, and their daily lives around horse husbandry. The Mongolian language has dozens of words for horses of different colors, gaits, and temperaments. Contemporary Mongolian and Kazakh nomadic cultures still maintain horse herding as a cultural practice with spiritual significance, even as the economic functions of horses have been largely replaced by mechanized alternatives.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Threat from the Steppe
comparativeNarrative.content: From the perspective of the settled civilizations of China, Persia, India, and medieval Europe, the horse-riding peoples of the steppe represented a recurring and terrifying military threat. The Great Wall of China was built to defend against horse-riding nomads. The Persian royal roads were designed partly to enable rapid military response to steppe incursions. The medieval European castle represented a settled civilization's response to the mobility of mounted raiders. The threat from the steppe shaped the political and military institutions of every settled civilization bordering the Eurasian grasslands for three millennia -- until gunpowder weapons finally negated the steppe nomads' tactical advantages in the early modern period.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Horse Returns
comparativeNarrative.content: Horses had existed in the Americas during the Pleistocene and went extinct there approximately 10,000 years ago. Their reintroduction by Spanish colonizers after 1492 represented a return in genetic if not cultural terms. The speed with which horses spread through indigenous North American societies -- and the speed with which those societies adapted to horse culture -- suggests that the Great Plains environment was well suited for horses and that indigenous peoples were capable of rapid cultural innovation. The Comanche's creation of a horse-based empire within a generation of acquiring horses is the most dramatic example, but similar transformations occurred among the Lakota, Cheyenne, Crow, Blackfoot, and dozens of other Plains peoples.

rabbitHole.label: The Mongol Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Mongol Empire was fundamentally a horse empire -- its military effectiveness, its mobility, and its ability to project power across Eurasia all rested on the horse as the core military and logistical technology

rabbitHole.label: Mosquitoes
rabbitHole.reason: Like horses, mosquitoes demonstrate how a single animal species can reshape the political and demographic history of entire continents -- the comparison illuminates how material foundations work

rabbitHole.label: The Columbian Exchange
rabbitHole.reason: The reintroduction of horses to the Americas after 1492 was one of the most consequential elements of the Columbian Exchange -- transforming indigenous societies across the Great Plains within a generation

rabbitHole.label: The Silk Road
rabbitHole.reason: The Silk Road's operation depended on horses -- both as the military technology that enabled the steppe peoples who controlled its Central Asian sections and as the pack animals that carried goods along it

rabbitHole.label: The Indo-European language expansion
rabbitHole.reason: The spread of Proto-Indo-European languages across Eurasia -- producing English, Spanish, Russian, Persian, Sanskrit, Greek, and dozens of other languages -- is one of the most consequential cultural events in human history and is plausibly connected to horse domestication

rabbitHole.label: The Comanche Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Comanche created a horse-based empire on the Great Plains within a generation of acquiring horses -- the most dramatic American example of what horse culture can accomplish, deserving its own entry

reference.author: David Anthony
reference.title: The Horse, the Wheel, and Language: How Bronze-Age Riders from the Eurasian Steppes Shaped the Modern World
reference.annotation: The foundational modern synthesis of horse domestication and its linguistic consequences -- arguing that horse-riding steppe peoples spread Proto-Indo-European languages across Eurasia. Draws on archaeology, linguistics, and horse biology. Essential for anyone approaching the subject seriously.

reference.author: Pita Kelekna
reference.title: The Horse in Human History
reference.annotation: The most comprehensive account of horses across multiple civilizations and time periods -- covering the military, economic, religious, and cultural dimensions of the human-horse relationship from Bronze Age Eurasia to the modern world. More accessible than Anthony and broader in scope.

reference.author: Pekka Hamalainen
reference.title: The Comanche Empire
reference.annotation: The essential account of horse culture in the Americas -- presenting the Comanche as imperial actors who shaped the history of the southern plains as fundamentally as the Spanish colonial system. Winner of the Bancroft Prize; transformed the historiography of Native American history.

reference.author: Robert Drews
reference.title: The End of the Bronze Age: Changes in Warfare and the Catastrophe ca. 1200 BC
reference.annotation: The argument that the Bronze Age Collapse around 1200 BCE was driven by the defeat of chariot-based militaries by infantry using iron weapons -- the most influential account of how horse-based military technology shaped and then lost the Bronze Age world.

reference.author: Ann Hyland
reference.title: The Horse in the Ancient World
reference.annotation: Hyland's account of ancient horse husbandry, training, and military use draws on both archaeological and textual evidence to reconstruct how horses were actually managed and deployed in ancient warfare. Essential for the practical dimensions of ancient horse culture.

commerce.title: The Horse, the Wheel, and Language
commerce.author: David Anthony
commerce.note: The essential scholarly account -- comprehensive, accessible for non-specialists, and the book that established the modern understanding of horse domestication and its consequences

commerce.title: The Horse in Human History
commerce.author: Pita Kelekna
commerce.note: Broader and more accessible than Anthony -- the best single-volume account of the horse role across multiple civilizations and time periods

commerce.title: The Comanche Empire
commerce.author: Pekka Hamalainen
commerce.note: The most dramatic account of what horse culture can accomplish -- the Comanche transformation of the Great Plains within a generation of acquiring horses is one of history's great stories of rapid adaptation

commerce.title: War Horse -- Steven Spielberg (2011)
commerce.note: Spielberg's adaptation of Michael Morpurgo's novel follows a horse from Devon through the Western Front -- the last major conflict in which horses were militarily central, capturing the transition from horse to mechanized warfare

commerce.title: The End of the Bronze Age
commerce.author: Robert Drews
commerce.note: For readers interested in how horse-based chariot warfare shaped the Bronze Age and what happened when it ended -- an essential account of the relationship between military technology and civilizational collapse

popularCulture.title: War Horse -- Steven Spielberg (2011)
popularCulture.description: Spielberg's adaptation follows a horse from a Devon farm through the Western Front â€” the last major conflict in which horses were used in large numbers for military transport. The film captures the transition: the horse is still central to logistics while being made obsolete by mechanized warfare. Its power comes from using the horse as a viewpoint character to observe human violence from outside the political frameworks that motivate it.

popularCulture.title: Mongolian Naadam festival
popularCulture.description: The Naadam festival â€” held annually in July in Mongolia, recognized by UNESCO â€” centers on wrestling, archery, and horse racing, with children as jockeys racing across open steppe for up to 30 kilometers. Naadam preserves the skills of the nomadic horse culture that built the Mongol Empire. Horse racing and horsemanship remain central to Mongolian identity in ways continuous with a horse culture stretching back thousands of years.

popularCulture.title: The Comanche transformation of the Great Plains
popularCulture.description: When the Comanche acquired horses in the late 17th century, they transformed themselves within a generation from semi-sedentary farmers into the most militarily effective horse culture in the Americas. By the early 19th century they controlled approximately 240,000 square miles of the southern plains. This transformation is one of history's most dramatic examples of how a material foundation change can reorganize an entire society's political economy within a single generation.

popularCulture.title: The horse in Chinese Tang poetry
popularCulture.description: Chinese Tang poetry contains some of the most sustained literary treatment of the horse as military and political symbol â€” emblems of freedom, military power, longing for the northern frontier. The Tang emperor Xuanzong's famous horses, depicted in stone reliefs at his mausoleum, are among the most celebrated artistic treatments of specific horses in world art. The tradition reflects both the military centrality of horses and the significance of Central Asian breeds to Tang cosmopolitanism.

popularCulture.title: The Horses of San Marco -- Venice
popularCulture.description: The four bronze horses above the entrance to St. Mark's Basilica in Venice â€” looted from Constantinople during the Fourth Crusade in 1204, taken by Napoleon in 1797, returned after 1815 â€” are among the most symbolically loaded objects in Western art history. Their history traces the political history of the ancient and medieval Mediterranean. No object better illustrates how horses functioned as political symbols in the ancient world.
